examples.english: He said, вЂњO my people, there is not error in me, but I am a messenger from the Lord of the worlds.вЂќ

senses.gloss: to be valiant, to be brave, to be courageous

etymology.text: Uncertain. Traditionally taken to be a contracted pronunciation of either a verb Щ„ЩЋЩЉЩђШіЩЋ (layisa), whose ultimate origin is in turn unknown or unexplored, or of Щ„ЩЋШ§ (lДЃ, вЂњnotвЂќ) + an existential ШЈЩЋЩЉЩ’Ші (К”ays) that has a proposed etymology in Proto-Semitic *ys < *yt. This existential would be a native cognate to Hebrew Ч™ЦµЧ©ЧЃ (yД“ЕЎ) and Akkadian р’Њ‡ (/вЃ iЕЎuвЃ /, вЂњto have or own; to beвЂќ, also used copulatively), although Al-Jallad (2018) expresses doubt that it is anything more than a back-formation from the negative, in other words implying that Щ„ЩЋШ§ (lДЃ, вЂњnotвЂќ) + *ШЈЩЋЩЉЩ’Ші (*К”ays, existential) is just a folk etymology for Щ„ЩЋЩЉЩ’ШіЩЋ (laysa) from which ШЈЩЋЩЉЩ’Ші (К”ays) was created artificially. Al-Jallad instead proceeds to identify a form of Щ„ЩЋЩЉЩ’ШіЩЋ (laysa) in a Safaitic inscription dated between the first century BC and the fourth century AD, whose form, he says, invalidates the possibility of an origin in a verb Щ„ЩЋЩЉЩђШіЩЋ (layisa); he thereby argues that Щ„ЩЋЩЉЩ’ШіЩЋ (laysa) may either be a loan from Neo-Assyrian р’†·р’Ђёр’‹— (la-aЕЎ-ЕЎu /вЃ laЕЎЕЎuвЃ /, вЂњthere is notвЂќ) or from Taymanitic рђЄЃрђЄљрђЄЏ (lysВі /вЃ lДЃКѕis, lДЃyisвЃ /, вЂњnot existing or beingвЂќ).
